Rationale: No individual property exists; all resources are communally held and allocated via trophallaxis and collective behaviors (Hölldobler & Wilson, 1990).
Resource distribution occurs through pheromone-mediated coordination and division of labor, with no market exchange or price signals (Gordon, 2010).
Activities serve colony-level inclusive fitness via kin selection; no individual profit or reproductive gain for sterile workers (Hamilton, 1964).
Food and nutrients are fully redistributed colony-wide through trophallaxis, independent of individual contribution.
Colony mechanisms support all members, including brood, injured workers, and queens, via kin-selected provisioning.
Queen monopoly and haplodiploidy ensure high relatedness (r≈0.75 among sisters), though some raters emphasize positive clonal fidelity.
Genetic variation is limited by single-queen mating in many species; phenotypic plasticity in castes provides moderate functional diversity.
Colony-level persistence spans decades to centuries through superorganism stability and continuous brood production.
Intra-colony parasitism (e.g., worker-laid eggs) is strongly suppressed via worker policing and chemical cues; efficiency >95% in many systems.
Inter-colony competition is intense for territory/resources, but intra-colony competition is minimized by high relatedness.
Colony survival is prioritized over individuals via collective defense, resource storage, and immune function.
Colonies engineer nests, trails, and fungal gardens to modify environments for long-term stability.
Cuticular hydrocarbons enforce strict nestmate recognition; non-nestmates are aggressively excluded.
Hygienic behaviors, recruitment cascades, and pheromone overrides repair damage and restore homeostasis.
Worker policing, oophagy, and chemical detection suppress reproductive cheats with high efficiency.
Caste-based division of labor and task plasticity create functional modules resilient to localized loss.
Pheromone trails, nest architecture, and collective memory store colony-level environmental information.
Behavioral uniformity is enforced by chemical and genetic mechanisms with near-zero deviation.
Queen pheromones suppress alternative reproductive or organizational signals colony-wide.
Unauthorized reproduction or deviant behavior is immediately policed and eliminated.
No independent family units exist; all workers are integrated into the colony superorganism.
No analogs to variation in signaling or ritual; behaviors are rigidly constrained.
All behaviors and constructions are functional/adaptive with no individual expressive variation.
Exploration and problem-solving are collective and fitness-aligned, with no individual direction.
Young workers acquire task knowledge through chemical priming and social transmission.
Communication is monopoly-controlled via colony-specific pheromones and tactile signals.
Defense is prioritized with soldier castes and mobilization, though allocation shifts with needs.
Colonies maintain strict territoriality and xenophobia, with rare exceptions in supercolonies.
Alarm pheromones trigger rapid, colony-wide defensive responses to intruders.
Queen replacement is limited; most monogynous colonies decline on queen death, though some have regulated polygyne mechanisms.
Queen pheromones centralize reproductive control and worker orientation, akin to functional monopoly.
Ongoing policing removes cheaters; seasonal purges of males or surplus reproductives occur in some species.
Queen fecundity drives colony growth; all energy prioritizes brood production and replacement.
Dispersal is tightly regulated; nuptial flights are consensus-gated, workers non-migratory.
Chemical nestmate recognition enforces absolute discrimination against non-kin.
